EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms fired along and ahead of a cold front during the afternoon and evening hours on June 10. Weak instability and shear kept storms mostly below severe limits except for a few isolated severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a tree fell onto a house and power lines on Market Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of very strong arctic High Pressure drifted south across the Northern Plains on January 7th and 8th.  This ushered very cold air into the upper Ohio Valley.  Northwest winds of 10 to 20 mph accompanied the cold air and they combined to create very dangerous wind chills.  Wind chills dipped below minus 25 degrees during the early morning hours of January 8th over much of Northwest and North-Central Ohio.  Low temperatures on the morning of the 8th were well below zero all areas with -5 at Toledo and -7 at Mansfield.  Wind chills remained colder than minus 25 through mid morning and then climbed into the negative teens by early afternoon.  Schools across the area were closed on both the 7th and 8th because of the dangerous wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dipped to minus 25 degrees during the predawn hours of January 8th.  Wind chills remained at or below minus 25 through mid morning and then slowly climbed into the negative teens during the afternoon.  All of the schools in the county were closed because of the bitter cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From mid-January through early March of 2015, frigid and much below normal temperatures set the stage for ice jam development along rivers and creeks in the Lake Erie drainage basin. Nearly every climate station in the Cleveland WFO region saw their coldest February on record in 2015. As a result,  Lake Erie was 98% frozen by early March. The lake is the mouth for almost all of northern Ohio and Pennsylvania rivers and creeks. When the lake is ice covered, it becomes a barrier to the natural outflow of the rivers. |Going into March the area had a widespread and significant snowpack based on regional climatology. The snowpack was heaviest across the snowbelt where the average depth was two to three feet.  Elsewhere the snowpack had compacted to eight inches or less. A survey conducted by NWS Cleveland employees shortly before the thaw revealed the snow water equivalent in the snowpack to be one to two inches west and south of Cleveland, and three to five inches in the snowbelt to the east. |By the middle of March temperatures began to warm.  A result of the warming was that by the end of the second week of March the snowpack was mostly gone except in the snowbelt. The runoff from this snowmelt began to raise river levels and break up the ice sheets that had accumulated along most northern Ohio and Pennsylvania Rivers. Numerous ice jams formed during this time, however no flooding impacts were reported. |A rain event moved along the Ohio River on the 13th resulting in rapid runoff in the region. The rainfall was not substantial in the Lake Erie basins, with amounts near a trace in the Maumee River near Toledo, to three quarters of an inch in the Cuyahoga Basin near Cleveland. Though not significant, the rainfall was sufficient enough to raise river levels. For those rivers experiencing ice jams, the increased flow was forced to back up and out of the river banks. The ice jams shifted and moved between the 13th through the 18th until the rivers were able to empty into Lake Erie. During that time numerous communities were impacted by the ice jams. Below is a summary of some of those communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 12th an ice jam formed on the Maumee River in Maumee Ohio. The river was frozen from Maumee to the mouth of the river at Lake Erie. The ice jam and subsequent water rises caused the Side Cut Metropark to close and experience record flooding. The road between Jerome Road and an access road east of Riverside Cemetery was damaged by the moving ice and needed repairs. In Maumee the Side Cut Metropark sustained around $50,000 in damages. The Riverside Cemetery is estimated to have sustained $1 million in damages. In Monclova the ice jams damaged two homes on North River Road.   These homes sustained damage from the ice to their decks and exterior. Only one of the two homes had water inundation into the interior.  The waste water treatment plant was impacted by the ice and rising river levels and had to bring in pumps to keep operations going.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the arctic deep freeze at dawn on the 20th, snow overspread southeast Ohio between 0300E and 0500E on the 21st.   New snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were common in 12 hours.  A few public reports of a 8 to 9 inch accumulation were received in Athens and southern Washington Counties.  In southern portions of both Lawrence and Gallia Counties, the snow changed to freezing rain during the morning, then to mostly rain by midday. The snow and rain diminished to drizzle by evening.  A quarter inch of ice from freezing rain was reported around Ironton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In less than a week, a second arctic front swept through southeast Ohio during the late morning hours of the 18th.  Snow showers formed ahead of the front, with a few bands lingering during the afternoon in its wake.  Snow accumulations of 2 to 3 inches were common in Jackson, Vinton, Athens, Perry, and Morgan Counties, with less toward the Ohio River.  ||Temperatures dropped into the zero to 5 below range by dawn on the 19th.   Despite sunshine through icy low clouds, daytime readings only recovered into the 5 to 10 degree range.  Wind chill readings of minus 10 to minus 20 were felt on the 19th. ||The diminishing winds and a clear sky developed first over southern counties then moved north during the overnight hours of the 19th into the 20th. With a fresh snow pack, temperatures dropped well below zero for dawn on Friday, the 20th.  The coldest official temperature in southeast Ohio was 26 degrees below zero at Waterloo of Lawrence County.  Readings of minus 15 to minus 20 were more common.  New Lexington had minus 17, South Point observed minus 15, Gallipolis had minus 13, and Jackson felt minus 12.  Unofficially, the Athens airport near Albany had minus 20 and the Scalia Lab at Ohio University dropped to minus 18.  The general public reported minus 20 in McArthur and around Kitts Hill. |  |In several counties, the morning of Friday the 20th was the coldest since the cold waves of February 1996 and January 1994.||A power outage on the 20th affected about 2,000 customers in Lawrence County.  It caused a pump at a water utility to fail.  About 800 customers in the Proctorville and Scottown vicinity lost their water service.

An upper level trough of low pressure in the Great Lakes region combined with a surface trough of low pressure on the western side of the Appalachians to transition rain in the area to snow during the evening hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EMA measured 4 inches of snow in Rome. A public report from Winchester noted 3 inches of snow had fallen. A spotter east of Seaman measured 2.8 inches, while the county garage near West Union measured 2.5 inches of snow.

An arctic air mass moved across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland on the 24th. Temperatures were well below zero with record lows across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low temperature of -19 was recorded at Sugarcreek. A record low temperature of -15 was set at New Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Streams and rivers were still swollen from previous rains and snow melt.  The Ohio River was still having some minor overflow as more rain began during the afternoon of the 13th.  This rain was associated with a warm front lifting north from Tennessee and southern Kentucky.  The rain diminished during the morning of the 14th.  Total rain amounts were just over an inch.  Minor stream flooding occurred in Lawrence and Meigs Counties.  Minor flooding also occurred along the swollen Ohio River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor small stream flooding occurred. Leading Creek flooded Route 124 near Langsville.  Route 681 was flooded by the headwaters of the West Branch of the Shade River.

Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of a weak cold front as it crossed the upper Ohio Valley. With weak upper level support, only a few storms became severe but with little steering flow, some storms produced heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stillwater creek reportedly came out of it's banks and flooded a residence near Point Breeze Ridge. About 2 feet of water flowed into yards.

After the arctic deep freeze at dawn on the 20th, snow overspread southeast Ohio between 0300E and 0500E on the 21st.   New snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were common in 12 hours.  A few public reports of a 8 to 9 inch accumulation were received in Athens and southern Washington Counties.  In southern portions of both Lawrence and Gallia Counties, the snow changed to freezing rain during the morning, then to mostly rain by midday. The snow and rain diminished to drizzle by evening.  A quarter inch of ice from freezing rain was reported around Ironton.

Deepening low pressure tracking east through the northern Ohio Valley brought a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow to the region February 1st into early February 2nd. Snowfall totals ranged between 7 and 15 inches, which created significant disruptions to travel across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed during the late evening hours of January 31st and became heavy at times February 1st into early February 2nd. Total snow accumulations across the county generally ranged between 10 and 13 inches. Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph, combined with the falling snow, created reduced visibilities and blowing snow. Numerous events were cancelled across the region, along with reports of slide-offs and accidents due to snow covered and slick roads. There were also reports of road closures in rural areas as unplowed roads became impassible. The heavy, wet, nature of the snow also led to sporadic power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of very strong arctic High Pressure drifted south across the Northern Plains on January 7th and 8th.  This ushered very cold air into the upper Ohio Valley.  Northwest winds of 10 to 20 mph accompanied the cold air and they combined to create very dangerous wind chills.  Wind chills dipped below minus 25 degrees during the early morning hours of January 8th over much of Northwest and North-Central Ohio.  Low temperatures on the morning of the 8th were well below zero all areas with -5 at Toledo and -7 at Mansfield.  Wind chills remained colder than minus 25 through mid morning and then climbed into the negative teens by early afternoon.  Schools across the area were closed on both the 7th and 8th because of the dangerous wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dipped to minus 25 degrees during the predawn hours of January 8th.  Wind chills remained at or below minus 25 through mid morning and then slowly climbed into the negative teens during the afternoon.  A morning low of -5 was reported at the Findlay Airport and coldest wind chill there was -27.  All of the schools in the county were closed because of the bitter cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another arctic front swept through during the early afternoon of the 14th.  Temperatures dropped from the low and mid 30s into the teens in a few hours.  In the wake of the front, wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were common into the night.  A burst of snow occurred along the front.  Accumulations were mostly an inch or less.||Temperatures dropped into the zero to 5 above range by dawn on the 15th.  For example, both New Lexington and Newport observed zero degrees.||Early on the 15th, wind chill readings of minus 10 to minus 15 were common.

An area of arctic high pressure moved from Canada to the western Great Lakes on February 15th.  Very cold air surged into northern Ohio in response to this high and was accompanied by strong north to northwest winds.  The combination of sub zero temperatures and winds gusting to as much as 30 mph created very dangerous wind chills over most of northern Ohio.  Wind chill readings dipped below minus 25 degrees during the predawn hours of 15th and then continued for four to six hours.  By midday, wind chills were back into the negative teens with slow improvement the remainder of the day.  Winds finally lessened during the evening hours as the high approached from the northwest.  By daybreak on the 16th, the high was centered over the Upper Ohio Valley resulting in clear skies and another frigid night.  Lows on the 15th were mainly 5 to 10 degrees below zero but most of the area dipped below minus 10 on the 16th.  The coldest wind chill reported was minus 30 at Toledo Express Airport (Lucas County) with minus 29 at the Northeast Ohio Regional Airport in Ashtabula County.  Fortunately all of the schools in northern Ohio were closed on the 16th for the Presidents Day Holiday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub zero temperatures and winds gusting to as much as 30 mph caused winds chills to get as cold as minus 30 degrees during the morning of February 15th. Winds lessened during the evening of the 15th allowing low temperatures on the 16th to dip to minus 10 or colder most areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the Central Plains early on February 1st.  The low then moved rapidly northeast toward the Ohio Valley eventually crossing southern Ohio during the evening of the 1st and early morning hours of the 2nd.  Snow associated with the low started in northwestern Ohio around midnight on the 1st.  By daybreak the snow had spread to the northeastern corner of the state.   The snow was initially light with accumulations of a couple tenths an hour, but after daybreak the snow intensified.  By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour.  The snow lessened toward evening as warmer air spread north into the region ahead of the low.  The snow changed or mixed with freezing rain or sleet for a time south and southeast of Cleveland during the late evening and early morning hours before switching back to all snow.  In Northwest Ohio the snow tapered off during the morning of the 2nd but another round of moderate to heavy snow occurred further east as wrap around moisture on the backside of the low reached the region.  The snow ended in the Cleveland area by mid afternoon and in far northeastern Ohio by early evening.  Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting.   Six or more inches of snow fell along and north of a line from near Findlay to Akron to just north of Youngstown.  A swath of a foot of more of snow stretched from Bowling Green in Wood County eastward along the south shore of Lake Erie and into Northeast Ohio.  A few of the higher totals included: 15.5 inches near Waterville in Lucas County, 12.2 inches at Toledo Express Airport also in Lucas County: 13.5 inches northeast of Bowling Green in Wood County; 14.0 inches south of Oak Harbor in Sandusky County; 11.0 inches in Ottawa County; 12.0 inches at Milan in Erie County; 12.0 inches at Bellevue in Huron County, 11.5 inches south of Lorain; 13.7 inches at Beachwood in Cuyahoga County; 12.0 inches at South Madison in Geauga County; 13.3 inches near Kirtland in Lake County; 12.6 inches at Pierpoint in Ashtabula County; 9.3 inches at Twinsburg in Summit County;  8.9 inches near Streetsboro in Portage County and 8.7 inches at Newton Falls in Trumbull County. In addition, a coating of ice was reported from Cleveland east and south.  Travel was severely disrupted by this storm.   Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times.  Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd. This was the biggest snow storm in several years for most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started around midnight on the 1st. The snow was initially light with accumulations of a couple tenths an hour, but after daybreak the snow intensified. By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour. In Northwest Ohio the snow tapered off during the morning of the 2nd. Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm. This caused considerable blowing and drifting.  A peak total of 13.5 inches was measured northeast of Bowling Green. Travel was severely disrupted by this storm. Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times. Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd.

By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour.  The snow lessened toward evening as warmer air spread north into the region ahead of the low.  The snow changed or mixed with freezing rain or sleet for a time south and southeast of Cleveland during the late evening and early morning hours before switching back to all snow.  In Northwest Ohio the snow tapered off during the morning of the 2nd but another round of moderate to heavy snow occurred further east as wrap around moisture on the backside of the low reached the region.  The snow ended in the Cleveland area by mid afternoon and in far northeastern Ohio by early evening.  Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting.   Six or more inches of snow fell along and north of a line from near Findlay to Akron to just north of Youngstown.  A swath of a foot of more of snow stretched from Bowling Green in Wood County eastward along the south shore of Lake Erie and into Northeast Ohio.  A few of the higher totals included: 15.5 inches near Waterville in Lucas County, 12.2 inches at Toledo Express Airport also in Lucas County: 13.5 inches northeast of Bowling Green in Wood County; 14.0 inches south of Oak Harbor in Sandusky County; 11.0 inches in Ottawa County; 12.0 inches at Milan in Erie County; 12.0 inches at Bellevue in Huron County, 11.5 inches south of Lorain; 13.7 inches at Beachwood in Cuyahoga County; 12.0 inches at South Madison in Geauga County; 13.3 inches near Kirtland in Lake County; 12.6 inches at Pierpoint in Ashtabula County; 9.3 inches at Twinsburg in Summit County;  8.9 inches near Streetsboro in Portage County and 8.7 inches at Newton Falls in Trumbull County. In addition, a coating of ice was reported from Cleveland east and south.  Travel was severely disrupted by this storm.   Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times.  Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd. This was the biggest snow storm in several years for most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started around midnight on the 1st. The snow was initially light with accumulations of a couple tenths an hour, but after daybreak the snow intensified. By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour. In Northwest Ohio the snow tapered off during the morning of the 2nd. Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm. This caused considerable blowing and drifting.  A few of the higher totals in the county included 15.5 inches near Waterville in Lucas County and 12.2 inches at Toledo Express Airport. Travel was severely disrupted by this storm. Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times. Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well in advance of a surface cold front, afternoon heating had temperatures in the mid and upper 80s. Dew points were in the mid 60s.  Clusters of thunderstorms formed and moved through southeast Ohio during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down along County Route 53.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms existed at dawn on the 8th in Indiana.  With support for the mid and upper level winds, this mesoscale convective complex maintained its intensity through the morning.  It raced southeast at 50 mph reaching southeast Ohio during the midday time frame.  Most areas saw brief wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph and rains of a quarter to a half inch.  A few areas saw stronger wind gusts.||After a lull, additional rounds of convection moved into southeast Ohio after sunset on the 8th.  Minor flooding occurred in Washington County as a result of the overnight convection.  Rain amounts of 1.5 to 2.5 inches occurred in 24 hours.||Distinct thunderstorm cells formed during the day on the 9th.  On the southern edge of a larger complex, one storm that moved from Athens County through southern Washington County had significant mid level rotation. Yet, no evidence of a tornado was found. ||Widespread showers accompanied the cold frontal passage early on the 10th.  Rain totals of 1 to 1.5 inches were reported that included both the afternoon convection on the 9th and the predawn showers on the 10th.||In the wake of the cold front, drier air finally moved into southeast Ohio during the daylight hours of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust estimated at 60 mph caused large limbs to break.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The larger streams and headwater rivers were above bank full, closing roads.  This included the Little Muskingum River at Bloomfield.  The river crested  around 20.4 feet during the late afternoon on the 9th, with bank full around 16 feet.  The stream gauge on Duck Creek at Whipple eventually crested on the 9th around 14.4 feet. This level was the highest level, since the gauge was installed back in 2012.  ||The additional rain early on the 10th, prolonged the minor flooding.  Rain of 1 to 1.25 inches was common along the front.

A few of the higher totals included: 15.5 inches near Waterville in Lucas County, 12.2 inches at Toledo Express Airport also in Lucas County: 13.5 inches northeast of Bowling Green in Wood County; 14.0 inches south of Oak Harbor in Sandusky County; 11.0 inches in Ottawa County; 12.0 inches at Milan in Erie County; 12.0 inches at Bellevue in Huron County, 11.5 inches south of Lorain; 13.7 inches at Beachwood in Cuyahoga County; 12.0 inches at South Madison in Geauga County; 13.3 inches near Kirtland in Lake County; 12.6 inches at Pierpoint in Ashtabula County; 9.3 inches at Twinsburg in Summit County;  8.9 inches near Streetsboro in Portage County and 8.7 inches at Newton Falls in Trumbull County. In addition, a coating of ice was reported from Cleveland east and south.  Travel was severely disrupted by this storm.   Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times.  Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd. This was the biggest snow storm in several years for most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started around midnight on the 1st. The snow was initially light with accumulations of a couple tenths an hour, but after daybreak the snow intensified. By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour. In Northwest Ohio the snow tapered off during the morning of the 2nd. Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm. This caused considerable blowing and drifting.  A peak total of 11.0 inches was reported in the county. Travel was severely disrupted by this storm. Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times. Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through southeast Ohio on the 3rd with a quarter to a half inch of rain. Late afternoon and evening temperatures rose into the 40s and 50s.  Winds and dew points also increased.  This combination helped accelerate the melting of the leftover snow pack.||Rains increased again overnight, with 1 to 1.7 inches of rain falling by dawn on the 4th in mainly north of the Hocking River Valley.  An automatic gauge near Lower Salem in Washington County measured 1.69 inches. Beverly had 1.48 inches of rain through dawn. This caused small stream flooding to begin.||Small stream flooding became more common along the Ohio River counties as a steady rain fell.  Rain rates were mostly 1 to 2 tenths of an inch per hour.  Total rainfall of 1.5 to 2 inches became common by that evening.||As the small stream flooding continued, the rain changed to sleet and wet snow during the late afternoon and early evening of the 4th.  Heavy wet snow quickly accumulated along the Ohio River counties during the late evening on the 4th and into the morning hours of Thursday the 5th. As the heavy snow was falling, several high water signs and barricades were stolen across Lawrence County.  Further north, the snow was lighter over Morgan and Perry Counties.||The snow diminished by early afternoon of the 5th.  A total snow accumulation of 10 to 13 inches was common from Jackson and Lawrence Counties on up the Ohio River to Washington County. For example, Waterloo of northern Lawrence County measured a 13 inch accumulation.  The snow depth at Gallipolis also went from zero to 13 inches during the storm.  Marietta reported a snow accumulation of a foot.  Further north snow accumulations were mostly 4 to 8 inches across Morgan, and 3 to 5 inches in Perry County.  An unofficial report of 17 inches was received north of Waterloo near the border with Gallia County.  A spotter in Thurman of Gallia County measured 14 inches of snow.||After transitioning from rain to snow, the wet snow accumulated on trees, especially evergreen trees.  Prolonged power outages were common in the counties adjacent to the Ohio River. ||After the storm, clearing resulted in a cold dawn on the 6th for so late in the season.  The coldest minimum temperatures was 8 below zero at New Lexington of Perry County.  Waterloo of Lawrence County had 6 below zero, while Jackson had minus 3.  Near the Ohio River readings were closer to zero degrees.  Newport in Washington County was 2 below zero. ||Eventually, the Ohio River had minor flooding from Gallia County on down the river through Ironton on the 6th into the 7th.  Overall, both the stream and river flooding mainly blocked roads with little damage to structures.

Near calm winds, a clear sky, and a snow cover allowed early morning temperatures to drop below zero.||The coldest official temperature was 15 below zero at New Lexington of Perry County.  Jackson was minus 9, Newport and Waterloo were minus 7.  Even Gallipolis was 3 below zero.||The Scalia Lab on the Ohio University campus in Athens, dropped to minus 11.||Public schools were canceled or on a 2 hour delayed start for the day.

A fast moving clipper system moved through on the 6th.  It was aided by a wind speed maximum in the flow aloft.  Surface temperatures were only in the upper teens to lower 20s.||Three to 5 inches of dry snow accumulated in less than 12 hours over a portion of southeast Ohio on the 6th.  This was mainly along the Route 50 corridor and north.  The water content was 2 to 3 tenths of an inch.  This was the first accumulating snow storm of the winter for these counties. ||Further south, snow amounts were much less in southeast Ohio.

A warm front lifted northeast through the region during the early morning hours. Heavy snow fell north of a Dayton to Columbus line with a mix of rain and snow significantly cutting totals along and southeast of the I-71 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 4 miles west of Rockbridge measured 3.1 inches of snow. Law enforcement in Logan measured 2.5 inches, while a social media report from Rockbridge indicated 2 inches of snow had fallen there.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the Central Plains early on February 1st.  The low then moved rapidly northeast toward the Ohio Valley eventually crossing southern Ohio during the evening of the 1st and early morning hours of the 2nd.  Snow associated with the low started in northwestern Ohio around midnight on the 1st.  By daybreak the snow had spread to the northeastern corner of the state.   The snow was initially light with accumulations of a couple tenths an hour, but after daybreak the snow intensified.  By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour.  The snow lessened toward evening as warmer air spread north into the region ahead of the low.  The snow changed or mixed with freezing rain or sleet for a time south and southeast of Cleveland during the late evening and early morning hours before switching back to all snow.  In Northwest Ohio the snow tapered off during the morning of the 2nd but another round of moderate to heavy snow occurred further east as wrap around moisture on the backside of the low reached the region.  The snow ended in the Cleveland area by mid afternoon and in far northeastern Ohio by early evening.  Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting.   Six or more inches of snow fell along and north of a line from near Findlay to Akron to just north of Youngstown.  A swath of a foot of more of snow stretched from Bowling Green in Wood County eastward along the south shore of Lake Erie and into Northeast Ohio.  A few of the higher totals included: 15.5 inches near Waterville in Lucas County, 12.2 inches at Toledo Express Airport also in Lucas County: 13.5 inches northeast of Bowling Green in Wood County; 14.0 inches south of Oak Harbor in Sandusky County; 11.0 inches in Ottawa County; 12.0 inches at Milan in Erie County; 12.0 inches at Bellevue in Huron County, 11.5 inches south of Lorain; 13.7 inches at Beachwood in Cuyahoga County; 12.0 inches at South Madison in Geauga County; 13.3 inches near Kirtland in Lake County; 12.6 inches at Pierpoint in Ashtabula County; 9.3 inches at Twinsburg in Summit County;  8.9 inches near Streetsboro in Portage County and 8.7 inches at Newton Falls in Trumbull County. In addition, a coating of ice was reported from Cleveland east and south.  Travel was severely disrupted by this storm.   Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times.  Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd. This was the biggest snow storm in several years for most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started during the early morning hours of the 1st. The snow was initially light with accumulations of a couple tenths an hour, but after daybreak the snow intensified. By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour. The snow briefly changed to freezing rain for a few hours late on the 1st and early on the 2nd. The freezing rain then switched back to snow and finally ended during the early afternoon hours. Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm. This caused considerable blowing and drifting. A peak total of 9.3 inches was reported at Twinsburg along with a slight coating of ice. Travel was severely disrupted by this storm. Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the Central Plains early on February 1st.  The low then moved rapidly northeast toward the Ohio Valley eventually crossing southern Ohio during the evening of the 1st and early morning hours of the 2nd.  Snow associated with the low started in northwestern Ohio around midnight on the 1st.  By daybreak the snow had spread to the northeastern corner of the state.   The snow was initially light with accumulations of a couple tenths an hour, but after daybreak the snow intensified.  By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour.  The snow lessened toward evening as warmer air spread north into the region ahead of the low.  The snow changed or mixed with freezing rain or sleet for a time south and southeast of Cleveland during the late evening and early morning hours before switching back to all snow.  In Northwest Ohio the snow tapered off during the morning of the 2nd but another round of moderate to heavy snow occurred further east as wrap around moisture on the backside of the low reached the region.  The snow ended in the Cleveland area by mid afternoon and in far northeastern Ohio by early evening.  Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting.   Six or more inches of snow fell along and north of a line from near Findlay to Akron to just north of Youngstown.  A swath of a foot of more of snow stretched from Bowling Green in Wood County eastward along the south shore of Lake Erie and into Northeast Ohio.  A few of the higher totals included: 15.5 inches near Waterville in Lucas County, 12.2 inches at Toledo Express Airport also in Lucas County: 13.5 inches northeast of Bowling Green in Wood County; 14.0 inches south of Oak Harbor in Sandusky County; 11.0 inches in Ottawa County; 12.0 inches at Milan in Erie County; 12.0 inches at Bellevue in Huron County, 11.5 inches south of Lorain; 13.7 inches at Beachwood in Cuyahoga County; 12.0 inches at South Madison in Geauga County; 13.3 inches near Kirtland in Lake County; 12.6 inches at Pierpoint in Ashtabula County; 9.3 inches at Twinsburg in Summit County;  8.9 inches near Streetsboro in Portage County and 8.7 inches at Newton Falls in Trumbull County. In addition, a coating of ice was reported from Cleveland east and south.  Travel was severely disrupted by this storm.   Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times.  Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd. This was the biggest snow storm in several years for most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started during the early morning hours of the 1st. The snow was initially light with accumulations of a couple tenths an hour, but after daybreak the snow intensified. By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour. The snow briefly changed to freezing rain and then rain for a few hours late on the 1st and early on the 2nd, especially across the southern half of the county. The rain then switched back to snow before daybreak and finally ended during the early evening hours. Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm. This caused considerable blowing and drifting. A peak total of 12.0 inches was reported at South Madison. Travel was severely disrupted by this storm. Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd.

The snow changed or mixed with freezing rain or sleet for a time south and southeast of Cleveland during the late evening and early morning hours before switching back to all snow.  In Northwest Ohio the snow tapered off during the morning of the 2nd but another round of moderate to heavy snow occurred further east as wrap around moisture on the backside of the low reached the region.  The snow ended in the Cleveland area by mid afternoon and in far northeastern Ohio by early evening.  Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting.   Six or more inches of snow fell along and north of a line from near Findlay to Akron to just north of Youngstown.  A swath of a foot of more of snow stretched from Bowling Green in Wood County eastward along the south shore of Lake Erie and into Northeast Ohio.  A few of the higher totals included: 15.5 inches near Waterville in Lucas County, 12.2 inches at Toledo Express Airport also in Lucas County: 13.5 inches northeast of Bowling Green in Wood County; 14.0 inches south of Oak Harbor in Sandusky County; 11.0 inches in Ottawa County; 12.0 inches at Milan in Erie County; 12.0 inches at Bellevue in Huron County, 11.5 inches south of Lorain; 13.7 inches at Beachwood in Cuyahoga County; 12.0 inches at South Madison in Geauga County; 13.3 inches near Kirtland in Lake County; 12.6 inches at Pierpoint in Ashtabula County; 9.3 inches at Twinsburg in Summit County;  8.9 inches near Streetsboro in Portage County and 8.7 inches at Newton Falls in Trumbull County. In addition, a coating of ice was reported from Cleveland east and south.  Travel was severely disrupted by this storm.   Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times.  Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd. This was the biggest snow storm in several years for most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started during the early morning hours of the 1st. The snow was initially light with accumulations of a couple tenths an hour, but after daybreak the snow intensified. By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour. Periods of light to moderate snow continued on the 2nd with the snow finally ending during the early evening hours. Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm. This caused considerable blowing and drifting. A peak total of 10.0 inches of snow was reported at Conneaut. Travel was severely disrupted by this storm. Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times. Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steady rain began near dawn on the 10th in southeast Ohio.  This was associated with a warm front.  By late afternoon, rain amounts over an inch were becoming common in Gallia and Lawrence Counties.  Less rain fell further to the north. Several small streams were still running well above normal before the rain even began.  Minor flooding along small streams occurred in the usual flood prone areas along roads.  Final rainfall totals by late night were mostly 1.2 to 1.4 inches in Lawrence and Gallia Counties lowering into the 0.75 to an inch range in Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pine Creek flooded Route 93 in the evening on the 10th. Other routes such as 141 and Arabia to Greasy Ridge Road were closed in spots.  Symmes Creek steadily rose to near 20 feet at Aid during the day on the 11th.  This was a slightly lower level than what occurred on the 4th into the 5th of the previous week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted over from 40 to over 50 MPH across portions of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania the afternoon and evening of the 21st. These winds occurred in a tight pressure gradient behind a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 45 MPH wind gust was measure at the Zanesville Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the arctic deep freeze at dawn on the 20th, snow overspread southeast Ohio between 0300E and 0500E on the 21st.   New snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were common in 12 hours.  A few public reports of a 8 to 9 inch accumulation were received in Athens and southern Washington Counties.  In southern portions of both Lawrence and Gallia Counties, the snow changed to freezing rain during the morning, then to mostly rain by midday. The snow and rain diminished to drizzle by evening.  A quarter inch of ice from freezing rain was reported around Ironton.

Another arctic front swept through during the early afternoon of the 14th.  Temperatures dropped from the low and mid 30s into the teens in a few hours.  In the wake of the front, wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were common into the night.  A burst of snow occurred along the front.  Accumulations were mostly an inch or less.||Temperatures dropped into the zero to 5 above range by dawn on the 15th.  For example, both New Lexington and Newport observed zero degrees.||Early on the 15th, wind chill readings of minus 10 to minus 15 were common.

A warm front lifted north through southeast Ohio on the 3rd with a quarter to a half inch of rain. Late afternoon and evening temperatures rose into the 40s and 50s.  Winds and dew points also increased.  This combination helped accelerate the melting of the leftover snow pack.||Rains increased again overnight, with 1 to 1.7 inches of rain falling by dawn on the 4th in mainly north of the Hocking River Valley.  An automatic gauge near Lower Salem in Washington County measured 1.69 inches. Beverly had 1.48 inches of rain through dawn. This caused small stream flooding to begin.||Small stream flooding became more common along the Ohio River counties as a steady rain fell.  Rain rates were mostly 1 to 2 tenths of an inch per hour.  Total rainfall of 1.5 to 2 inches became common by that evening.||As the small stream flooding continued, the rain changed to sleet and wet snow during the late afternoon and early evening of the 4th.  Heavy wet snow quickly accumulated along the Ohio River counties during the late evening on the 4th and into the morning hours of Thursday the 5th. As the heavy snow was falling, several high water signs and barricades were stolen across Lawrence County.  Further north, the snow was lighter over Morgan and Perry Counties.||The snow diminished by early afternoon of the 5th.  A total snow accumulation of 10 to 13 inches was common from Jackson and Lawrence Counties on up the Ohio River to Washington County. For example, Waterloo of northern Lawrence County measured a 13 inch accumulation.  The snow depth at Gallipolis also went from zero to 13 inches during the storm.  Marietta reported a snow accumulation of a foot.  Further north snow accumulations were mostly 4 to 8 inches across Morgan, and 3 to 5 inches in Perry County.  An unofficial report of 17 inches was received north of Waterloo near the border with Gallia County.  A spotter in Thurman of Gallia County measured 14 inches of snow.||After transitioning from rain to snow, the wet snow accumulated on trees, especially evergreen trees.  Prolonged power outages were common in the counties adjacent to the Ohio River. ||After the storm, clearing resulted in a cold dawn on the 6th for so late in the season.  The coldest minimum temperatures was 8 below zero at New Lexington of Perry County.  Waterloo of Lawrence County had 6 below zero, while Jackson had minus 3.  Near the Ohio River readings were closer to zero degrees.  Newport in Washington County was 2 below zero. ||Eventually, the Ohio River had minor flooding from Gallia County on down the river through Ironton on the 6th into the 7th.  Overall, both the stream and river flooding mainly blocked roads with little damage to structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the Central Plains early on February 1st.  The low then moved rapidly northeast toward the Ohio Valley eventually crossing southern Ohio during the evening of the 1st and early morning hours of the 2nd.  Snow associated with the low started in northwestern Ohio around midnight on the 1st.  By daybreak the snow had spread to the northeastern corner of the state.   The snow was initially light with accumulations of a couple tenths an hour, but after daybreak the snow intensified.  By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour.  The snow lessened toward evening as warmer air spread north into the region ahead of the low.  The snow changed or mixed with freezing rain or sleet for a time south and southeast of Cleveland during the late evening and early morning hours before switching back to all snow.  In Northwest Ohio the snow tapered off during the morning of the 2nd but another round of moderate to heavy snow occurred further east as wrap around moisture on the backside of the low reached the region.  The snow ended in the Cleveland area by mid afternoon and in far northeastern Ohio by early evening.  Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting.   Six or more inches of snow fell along and north of a line from near Findlay to Akron to just north of Youngstown.  A swath of a foot of more of snow stretched from Bowling Green in Wood County eastward along the south shore of Lake Erie and into Northeast Ohio.  A few of the higher totals included: 15.5 inches near Waterville in Lucas County, 12.2 inches at Toledo Express Airport also in Lucas County: 13.5 inches northeast of Bowling Green in Wood County; 14.0 inches south of Oak Harbor in Sandusky County; 11.0 inches in Ottawa County; 12.0 inches at Milan in Erie County; 12.0 inches at Bellevue in Huron County, 11.5 inches south of Lorain; 13.7 inches at Beachwood in Cuyahoga County; 12.0 inches at South Madison in Geauga County; 13.3 inches near Kirtland in Lake County; 12.6 inches at Pierpoint in Ashtabula County; 9.3 inches at Twinsburg in Summit County;  8.9 inches near Streetsboro in Portage County and 8.7 inches at Newton Falls in Trumbull County. In addition, a coating of ice was reported from Cleveland east and south.  Travel was severely disrupted by this storm.   Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times.  Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd. This was the biggest snow storm in several years for most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started during the early morning hours of the 1st. The snow was initially light with accumulations of a couple tenths an hour, but after daybreak the snow intensified. By mid morning visibilities were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of an inch or more per hour. Periods of light to moderate snow continued on the 2nd with the snow finally ending during the early evening hours. Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm. This caused considerable blowing and drifting. A peak total of 12.6 inches of snow was reported at Pierpont. Travel was severely disrupted by this storm. Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times. Schools were closed over most of the area on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of arctic high pressure moved from Canada to the western Great Lakes on February 15th.  Very cold air surged into northern Ohio in response to this high and was accompanied by strong north to northwest winds.  The combination of sub zero temperatures and winds gusting to as much as 30 mph created very dangerous wind chills over most of northern Ohio.  Wind chill readings dipped below minus 25 degrees during the predawn hours of 15th and then continued for four to six hours.  By midday, wind chills were back into the negative teens with slow improvement the remainder of the day.  Winds finally lessened during the evening hours as the high approached from the northwest.  By daybreak on the 16th, the high was centered over the Upper Ohio Valley resulting in clear skies and another frigid night.  Lows on the 15th were mainly 5 to 10 degrees below zero but most of the area dipped below minus 10 on the 16th.  The coldest wind chill reported was minus 30 at Toledo Express Airport (Lucas County) with minus 29 at the Northeast Ohio Regional Airport in Ashtabula County.  Fortunately all of the schools in northern Ohio were closed on the 16th for the Presidents Day Holiday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub zero temperatures and winds gusting to as much as 30 mph caused winds chills to get as cold as minus 30 degrees during the morning of February 15th. Winds lessened during the evening of the 15th allowing low temperatures on the 16th to dip to minus 10 or colder most areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms existed at dawn on the 8th in Indiana.  With support for the mid and upper level winds, this mesoscale convective complex maintained its intensity through the morning.  It raced southeast at 50 mph reaching southeast Ohio during the midday time frame.  Most areas saw brief wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph and rains of a quarter to a half inch.  A few areas saw stronger wind gusts.||After a lull, additional rounds of convection moved into southeast Ohio after sunset on the 8th.  Minor flooding occurred in Washington County as a result of the overnight convection.  Rain amounts of 1.5 to 2.5 inches occurred in 24 hours.||Distinct thunderstorm cells formed during the day on the 9th.  On the southern edge of a larger complex, one storm that moved from Athens County through southern Washington County had significant mid level rotation. Yet, no evidence of a tornado was found. ||Widespread showers accompanied the cold frontal passage early on the 10th.  Rain totals of 1 to 1.5 inches were reported that included both the afternoon convection on the 9th and the predawn showers on the 10th.||In the wake of the cold front, drier air finally moved into southeast Ohio during the daylight hours of the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system tracked northeast from southern Lake Michigan to eastern upper Michigan during the early morning hours of April 10.  This system pushed a cold front east across the area with isolated wind gusts of 50 knots or more occurring along and behind the front.  A wind gust to 52 knots was recorded at the Findlay Airport. At the same time strong wind gusts caused a tree to fall on a house in North Baltimore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Findlay Airport recorded a gust to 52 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving southeast from the eastern Great Lakes through the Upper Ohio Valley produced widespread showers and thunderstorms. Some of the thunderstorms were severe with damaging winds most commonly reported. The thunderstorms also produced torrential rainfall at times, with flash flooding reported across portions of eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported Plum Run Road washed out north of SR 250.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms existed at dawn on the 8th in Indiana.  With support for the mid and upper level winds, this mesoscale convective complex maintained its intensity through the morning.  It raced southeast at 50 mph reaching southeast Ohio during the midday time frame.  Most areas saw brief wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph and rains of a quarter to a half inch.  A few areas saw stronger wind gusts.||After a lull, additional rounds of convection moved into southeast Ohio after sunset on the 8th.  Minor flooding occurred in Washington County as a result of the overnight convection.  Rain amounts of 1.5 to 2.5 inches occurred in 24 hours.||Distinct thunderstorm cells formed during the day on the 9th.  On the southern edge of a larger complex, one storm that moved from Athens County through southern Washington County had significant mid level rotation. Yet, no evidence of a tornado was found. ||Widespread showers accompanied the cold frontal passage early on the 10th.  Rain totals of 1 to 1.5 inches were reported that included both the afternoon convection on the 9th and the predawn showers on the 10th.||In the wake of the cold front, drier air finally moved into southeast Ohio during the daylight hours of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail as large as golf balls fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of light freezing rain in advance of an approaching warm front created slick roadways in time for the morning commute on March 3rd. Ice accretions of around a tenth of an inch were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of Light freezing rain with temperatures in the mid to upper 20s created slick spots on area roads during the morning hours of March 3rd. These icy conditions led to numerous school closings and minor accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow behind a departing arctic front pulled a significant amount of moisture over the Ohio Valley Friday night, February 20th into Saturday the 21st. As the low level jet encountered a mid level disturbance, snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were noted over much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three an a half inches of snow fell at the county garage south of Wapakoneta.

Six to twelve inches of heavy snow was reported in several counties in OH, WV, MD and southern PA through early afternoon on the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From mid-January through early March of 2015, frigid and much below normal temperatures set the stage for ice jam development along rivers and creeks in the Lake Erie drainage basin. Nearly every climate station in the Cleveland WFO region saw their coldest February on record in 2015. As a result,  Lake Erie was 98% frozen by early March. The lake is the mouth for almost all of northern Ohio and Pennsylvania rivers and creeks. When the lake is ice covered, it becomes a barrier to the natural outflow of the rivers. |Going into March the area had a widespread and significant snowpack based on regional climatology. The snowpack was heaviest across the snowbelt where the average depth was two to three feet.  Elsewhere the snowpack had compacted to eight inches or less. A survey conducted by NWS Cleveland employees shortly before the thaw revealed the snow water equivalent in the snowpack to be one to two inches west and south of Cleveland, and three to five inches in the snowbelt to the east. |By the middle of March temperatures began to warm.  A result of the warming was that by the end of the second week of March the snowpack was mostly gone except in the snowbelt. The runoff from this snowmelt began to raise river levels and break up the ice sheets that had accumulated along most northern Ohio and Pennsylvania Rivers. Numerous ice jams formed during this time, however no flooding impacts were reported. |A rain event moved along the Ohio River on the 13th resulting in rapid runoff in the region. The rainfall was not substantial in the Lake Erie basins, with amounts near a trace in the Maumee River near Toledo, to three quarters of an inch in the Cuyahoga Basin near Cleveland. Though not significant, the rainfall was sufficient enough to raise river levels. For those rivers experiencing ice jams, the increased flow was forced to back up and out of the river banks. The ice jams shifted and moved between the 13th through the 18th until the rivers were able to empty into Lake Erie. During that time numerous communities were impacted by the ice jams. Below is a summary of some of those communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours of the 12th an ice jam developed on the Chagrin River in Willoughby Hills Ohio. The ice backed up in the southern loop of the Chagrin River just west of the Trailard Drive community.  The streets were first overtopped in southwest corner and north east of the Horseshoe Glen subdivision (Trailard Dr.).  About 20 people were voluntarily evacuated after the water levels doubled, rising to 15 feet.  This is the worst it's been since 1977-78, Chris Donovan, a longtime resident who lives on the east portion of Trailard Drive near the river. The initial assessment of the homes indicated marginal flood damage. The water levels did not reach the first floor of most of the homes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ice jam in Willoughby Hills broke during the early morning hours of the 14th. The water and ice came up fast in the West Island Drive community of Eastlake around 6 am. Around a dozen homes were evacuated. The damages were limited to docks and some low lying basements and garages. The community of Eastlake is especially vulnerable to ice jam flooding because they are at the confluence of the river and Lake Erie. Ice cutters were unable to move any ice at the mouth of the Chagrin River in Eastlake, which contributed to the severity of the ice jams. Despite the lake being ice covered, the river was able to recede by early afternoon on the 14th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first report of flooding in Fremont was on the afternoon of March 11th. The Emergency Manager reported an ice jam located near the Ballville Dam just south of town, which was causing some flooding along South River Road. The flooding cut off three homes from emergency access but did not enter any of the properties. The flooding lasted for several days as more snowmelt and rain kept the river levels of the Sandusky River high. The ice chunks broke down and began flowing over the dam by Monday the 16th. At that time, the river was able to recede.  Officials report a few water rescues were needed during the period of flood with no injuries or fatalities.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 45 mph and snow squalls along and behind an arctic front created near whiteout conditions at times on February 14th. Visibilities were reduced to less than 200 feet in heavier snow showers, with total snow accumulations generally ranging between 1 and 3 inches. Several multi-vehicle accidents were reported across the region due to reduced visibilities and slick roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey team found evidence of a weak tornado touching down near the intersection of Glenmina Drive and East Rahn Road. Significant tree damage consisting of snapped and uprooted trees was noted along much of the length of Glenmina Drive bounded by East Rahn Road and Wilmington Pike. In particular, many of the trees fell in a cross-path component to the storm's motion, suggesting the presence of ground-based circulation along or immediately west of Glenmina Drive. Little to no structural damage was noted in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon, a low level boundary existed along the Ohio River from near Cincinnati to Ironton, then east into central West Virginia.   Rotating thunderstorms formed along this boundary, the strongest of which moved east across interior Lawrence County. Downpours also occurred.  An automatic gauge near Kitts Hill measured 1.4 inches in an hour with 1.9 inches in less than 3 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and strong winds created near blizzard conditions at times on February 14th along and behind a strong arctic front. Snow amounts generally ranged between 1 and 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 45 mph and snow squalls along and behind an arctic front created near whiteout conditions at times on February 14th. Visibilities were reduced to less than 200 feet in heavier snow showers, with total snow accumulations generally ranging between 1 and 3 inches. Several multi-vehicle accidents were reported across the region due to reduced visibilities and slick roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed in central Ohio in response to a disturbance in the winds aloft. As the thunderstorms moved southeast, a diffuse line formed. A few storms pulsed to stronger levels.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Geauga County Sheriffs Office reported thunderstorms winds brought down a few trees on Secondary Roads in Russell Township.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed along the warm front, one of which developed into a severe thunderstorm that brought trees down in portions of eastern Trumbull and Mahoning counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast Media reported thunderstorm winds brought down a tree and power lines in Lowellville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures on May 26th climbed into the upper 80s.  An upper level disturbance then moved over the area causing showers and thunderstorms to develop.  A few of the thunderstorms became severe with gusts over 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper shortwave crossed the Ohio Valley late in the evening and overnight on the 26th. This helped develop showers and thunderstorms in an axis of instability, but weak shear supported mainly marginal strong to severe storms. Damage over eastern Ohio was mainly downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 center reported trees down on East Pike near Zane Grey Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms pushed slowly south across central Ohio during the late evening hours. The storms produced heavy rain and localized flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving southeast from the eastern Great Lakes through the Upper Ohio Valley produced widespread showers and thunderstorms. Some of the thunderstorms were severe with damaging winds most commonly reported. The thunderstorms also produced torrential rainfall at times, with flash flooding reported across portions of eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported trees down.

A line of severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front that pushed south across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed along Country Club Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours along a cold front moving through the Ohio Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the Gulf and Mid-Atlantic was pulled northward over the region through the 29th by an upper trough over the Lower Mississippi Valley and a cut-off low off of the Southeastern U.S. coast. This brought a period of heavy rain to the region before a cold front passed on the 30th. While the antecedent conditions were dry, over 3.50 inches of rain over portions of southwestern PA caused localized flooding of roadways and basements. 3.43 inches were reported at the Pittsburgh Airport, which was a new 24 hour record on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported that Wheeling Creek was out of it's banks and several roads were closed due to flooding throughout the county.

The first area of wind damage was located in Stark County near Navarre where thunderstorm winds brought down 8-10 trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers occurred on the 29th.  Rain amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell in less than 24 hours in Washington County.  For example, 3.59 inches of rain was measured in Beverly. These heavier rains also fell over portions of Noble and Morgan Counties and drained south, into Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of Duck Creek crested around 14 feet, or about a foot over bankfull in the vicinity of Macksburg.  This caused minor street flooding in the town.  Further down Duck Creek, Route 821 was flooded in places near Whipple.  The gauge near Whipple crested around 11.5 feet during the afternoon of the 30th. Bankfull at Whipple is 11 feet. ||The Little Muskingum River rose some 13 feet in the vertical and crested around 16.8 feet during the morning of the 30th. That is less than a foot above its 16 foot bankfull stage.  A few small sections of State Route 28 were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening of September 3rd ahead of an upper level disturbance moving southeast through the Great Lakes Region.  The airmass was moderately unstable but shear was weak.  During the evening, an area of storms in Michigan evolved into a line moved southeast into Ohio and across western Lake Erie.  These storms caused wind damage and hail in Lucas and Ottawa Counties.  Reports of wind damage and hail were both received elsewhere.  Minor flooding accompanied many of the storms due to their slow motion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported nickel size hail 5 miles south of Fremont.

The thunderstorms continued into the early morning hours of July 19th before tapering off. The storms produced locally torrential downpours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall in portions of northwestern Ohio. A combination of upstream rainfall in Fort Wayne, Indiana and rainfall across Putnam county set the stage for flash flooding in the town of Payne, forcing evacuation of parts of the town as several inches of rain fell both upstream in Indiana and over the area. A similar scenario took place in Deshler (Henry county) where a care facility had to be evacuated due to Brush Creek overflowing. One of the storms produced a wet microburst, causing some tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed due to high water for several days as periodic showers and storms slowed water levels from receding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of thunderstorms developed across northern Ohio ahead of a cold front and associated upper level low dropping south across the Great Lakes Region.  The airmass ahead of the front was moist and thunderstorms produced a combination of damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.  In Geauga County, heavy rainfall caused flash flooding from Bainbridge Township north to Novelty. Silver Creek came out of its banks in Novelty and flooded the Copaw Cabana Kennel where three people and 12 dogs had to be rescued around 4 pm. Another water rescue occurred on Snyder Road where a woman's car was moved by water. Rushing water entered the Chagrin Valley Roller Rink south of Chagrin Falls in Geauga County with rushing water of 3 to 4 feet deep on nearby Cedar and South Streets. Tanglewood Lake overflowed and reached the secondary spillway, also flooding the Tanglewood Golf Club causing damage to at least 2 holes. Lake Lucerne Dam was in danger of breaching and water overflowing the lake reached homes downstream with numerous flooded basements. One observation from South Russell indicated 2.91 inches of rain fell, mostly between 1 and 4 pm with most areas receiving at least 1.5 to almost 3 inches of rain. Flash flooding also occurred in Chagrin Falls area of Cuyahoga County.  Water was flowing from upstream in Geauga County to the Chagrin Falls area and residents reported a 6-foot wall of water moved through the neighborhood and destroyed the wall of a walk out basement on Elm Court. Numerous other basements in the area were flooded. Several roads had to be closed due to the flooding. Rapid rises occurred on the Chagrin River with the water running swiftly and at high capacity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding from Bainbridge Township north to Novelty. Silver Creek came out of its banks in Novelty and flooded the Copaw Cabana Kennel where three people and 12 dogs had to be rescued around 4 pm. Another water rescue occurred on Snyder Road where a woman's car was moved by water. Rushing water entered the Chagrin Valley Roller Rink south of Chagrin Falls in Geauga County with rushing water of 3 to 4 feet deep on nearby Cedar and South Streets. Numerous roads were closed in Bainbridge including Ohio Route 306, Haskins Road, Snyder Road under 422, Bainbridge Road and East Washington. Tanglewood Lake overflowed and reached the secondary spillway, also flooding the Tanglewood Golf Club causing damage to at least 2 holes. Lake Lucerne Dam was in danger of breaching and water overflowing the lake reached homes downstream with numerous flooded basements.   One observation from South Russell indicated 2.91 inches of rain fell, mostly between 1 and 4 pm. The Aurora Branch of the Chagrin River was overwhelmed and flooded area roads in northern Aurora including State Route 306, Treat Road, and Crackel Roads. The flowing water damaged some of the pavement which needed to be replaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The edge of strong high pressure was situated over northwestern Ohio as a series of disturbances moved into the region. In addition, the remnants of a tropical system added to already abundant moisture to set the stage for isolated severe storms and several rounds of torrential rainfall for parts of northwestern Ohio. Rainfall rates of two to three inches per hour were common with the stronger cells, allowing 5 day rain totals in some areas to exceed eight inches. Widespread high water was reported in several counties, with the town of Kalida being cut off for a period of time as several creeks and rivers all exceeded their banks at the same time, closing all roads into the town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported several tree limbs roughly one foot in diameter blown down. Smaller branches were also noted blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across Brown County around 2100EST. A rain gauge near Red Oak received 2.4 inches of rain between 2120EST and 2205EST. Flooding started in the Georgetown area around 2140EST, when 6 people were stranded in multiple vehicles along State Route 125 due to rapidly rising water on Straight Creek. Over the next hour, numerous water rescues occurred throughout much of Brown County. By 2220EST, 3.0 inches of rain had fallen at the Red Oak gauge. Between 2200EST  and 2300EST, Red Oak Creek rapidly rose out of its banks and caused widespread damage to homes and roads along the creek from Red Oak to where the creek enters the Ohio River in Ripley. Over 30 homes were affected by flood waters along Red Oak Creek. Three homes along the creek were completely destroyed and another 5 homes suffered major damage. Two adults and 4 children were in one of the houses as it was swept into the creek.  One adult and two of the children were killed and the others were injured.  Flash flooding continued into the early morning hours of the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system tracked northeast along a quasi-stationary boundary, bringing widespread showers and thunderstorms to northwest Ohio during the early morning hours of July 9th. Showers and thunderstorms were very efficient rainfall producers, with reports of 3 to 4 inches of rainfall reported in Van Wert county. This, combined with saturated ground conditions from previous rainfall, allowed for widespread areal flooding across Van Wert county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to nearly four inches of rain fell across the county, causing numerous high water problems. The highest report was 3.7 inches in Wetsel in a 24 hour period, with a 72 hour total of 4.99 inches being recorded near Middle Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The edge of strong high pressure was situated over northwestern Ohio as a series of disturbances moved into the region. In addition, the remnants of a tropical system added to already abundant moisture to set the stage for isolated severe storms and several rounds of torrential rainfall for parts of northwestern Ohio. Rainfall rates of two to three inches per hour were common with the stronger cells, allowing 5 day rain totals in some areas to exceed eight inches. Widespread high water was reported in several counties, with the town of Kalida being cut off for a period of time as several creeks and rivers all exceeded their banks at the same time, closing all roads into the town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water impacted many county and state routes at one point or another during the duration of the flood event. The Saint Marys river well exceeded its banks, especially in the town of Willshire, where flood waters impacted parts of the town for several days. Five to locally as much as eight inches of rain fell over mainly southern parts of the county onto already saturated ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The edge of strong high pressure was situated over northwestern Ohio as a series of disturbances moved into the region. In addition, the remnants of a tropical system added to already abundant moisture to set the stage for isolated severe storms and several rounds of torrential rainfall for parts of northwestern Ohio. Rainfall rates of two to three inches per hour were common with the stronger cells, allowing 5 day rain totals in some areas to exceed eight inches. Widespread high water was reported in several counties, with the town of Kalida being cut off for a period of time as several creeks and rivers all exceeded their banks at the same time, closing all roads into the town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall fell well south of the county across much of Allen county, Ohio which ran off into the Ottawa River. The runoff from this rainfall rapidly moved upstream into the county and caused a rapid increase in river and creek levels around the town of Kalida. High water impacted nearly the entire town, with some locations seeing several feet of flood water closer to the river edges. Of greater significance was that the high water levels caused flood waters to cover all of the state and U.S. routes that intersect within the town limits. This essentially cut the town off from the surrounding communities due to a combination of the high water on the road and travel of vehicles through the water causing wave action which increased local flooding issues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The edge of strong high pressure was situated over northwestern Ohio as a series of disturbances moved into the region. In addition, the remnants of a tropical system added to already abundant moisture to set the stage for isolated severe storms and several rounds of torrential rainfall for parts of northwestern Ohio. Rainfall rates of two to three inches per hour were common with the stronger cells, allowing 5 day rain totals in some areas to exceed eight inches. Widespread high water was reported in several counties, with the town of Kalida being cut off for a period of time as several creeks and rivers all exceeded their banks at the same time, closing all roads into the town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While overall rainfall amounts were not as widespread as other adjacent counties, what rain did fall only added to rising river and creek levels from upstream rainfall. Numerous township, county and state roads were impacted by high water, forcing high water signage or in some cases closures. No rescues or property damage was reported. Damage estimates for any roads was not available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The edge of strong high pressure was situated over northwestern Ohio as a series of disturbances moved into the region. In addition, the remnants of a tropical system added to already abundant moisture to set the stage for isolated severe storms and several rounds of torrential rainfall for parts of northwestern Ohio. Rainfall rates of two to three inches per hour were common with the stronger cells, allowing 5 day rain totals in some areas to exceed eight inches. Widespread high water was reported in several counties, with the town of Kalida being cut off for a period of time as several creeks and rivers all exceeded their banks at the same time, closing all roads into the town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the peak of the event, several dozen roads were partially or completely impassable from flowing or standing water as runoff from the heavy rain overwhelmed local streams, creeks and rivers. Portions of several State routes were closed for an extended period of time with 6 to 12 inches of water flowing over them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The edge of strong high pressure was situated over northwestern Ohio as a series of disturbances moved into the region. In addition, the remnants of a tropical system added to already abundant moisture to set the stage for isolated severe storms and several rounds of torrential rainfall for parts of northwestern Ohio. Rainfall rates of two to three inches per hour were common with the stronger cells, allowing 5 day rain totals in some areas to exceed eight inches. Widespread high water was reported in several counties, with the town of Kalida being cut off for a period of time as several creeks and rivers all exceeded their banks at the same time, closing all roads into the town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While overall rainfall amounts were not as significant as counties to the south of Putnam county, river and creek levels increased across mainly the southern portion of the county, causing several road closures due to high water. The most significant flooding occurred in the Kalida area, with a separate entry under Flash Flood outlining the details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of thunderstorms developed across northern Ohio ahead of a cold front and associated upper level low dropping south across the Great Lakes Region.  The airmass ahead of the front was moist and thunderstorms produced a combination of damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.  In Geauga County, heavy rainfall caused flash flooding from Bainbridge Township north to Novelty. Silver Creek came out of its banks in Novelty and flooded the Copaw Cabana Kennel where three people and 12 dogs had to be rescued around 4 pm. Another water rescue occurred on Snyder Road where a woman's car was moved by water. Rushing water entered the Chagrin Valley Roller Rink south of Chagrin Falls in Geauga County with rushing water of 3 to 4 feet deep on nearby Cedar and South Streets. Numerous roads were closed in Bainbridge including Ohio Route 306, Haskins Road, Snyder Road under 422, Bainbridge Road and East Washington. Tanglewood Lake overflowed and reached the secondary spillway, also flooding the Tanglewood Golf Club causing damage to at least 2 holes. Lake Lucerne Dam was in danger of breaching and water overflowing the lake reached homes downstream with numerous flooded basements. One observation from South Russell indicated 2.91 inches of rain fell, mostly between 1 and 4 pm with most areas receiving at least 1.5 to almost 3 inches of rain. Flash flooding also occurred in Chagrin Falls area of Cuyahoga County.  Water was flowing from upstream in Geauga County to the Chagrin Falls area and residents reported a 6-foot wall of water moved through the neighborhood and destroyed the wall of a walk out basement on Elm Court. Numerous other basements in the area were flooded. Several roads had to be closed due to the flooding. Rapid rises occurred on the Chagrin River with the water running swiftly and at high capacity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees in Clinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall in portions of northwestern Ohio. A combination of upstream rainfall in Fort Wayne, Indiana and rainfall across Putnam county set the stage for flash flooding in the town of Payne, forcing evacuation of parts of the town as several inches of rain fell both upstream in Indiana and over the area. A similar scenario took place in Deshler (Henry county) where a care facility had to be evacuated due to Brush Creek overflowing. One of the storms produced a wet microburst, causing some tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported a large tree down onto a local road. In addition, a tree fell onto an unoccupied car on Beckham Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convective complex drove southeastward our of Ohio ahead of approaching surface front on the 14th. While the strongest part of the line moved just south of our forecast area, a portion of the line brought damage to Noble and Harrison counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty to thirty hardwood trees were bent, snapped, and toppled on an acre of land just north of Scio. Owner of the land sent several pictures of trees snapped 30-50 feet off the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall in portions of northwestern Ohio. A combination of upstream rainfall in Fort Wayne, Indiana and rainfall across Putnam county set the stage for flash flooding in the town of Payne, forcing evacuation of parts of the town as several inches of rain fell both upstream in Indiana and over the area. A similar scenario took place in Deshler (Henry county) where a care facility had to be evacuated due to Brush Creek overflowing. One of the storms produced a wet microburst, causing some tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Residents of the town of Payne began experiencing flash flooding during the early afternoon hours of the 27th as Flat Rock Creek responded rapidly to upstream runoff in Indiana as well as several inches of rain over the area.  The threat increased the remainder of the day and into much of the 28th as several additional rounds of rain occurred. Numerous evacuations were needed along the creek in Payne and eventually about 8 miles downstream in Paulding where southern portions of the town were impacted. Several state and county roads were closed in the area due to the high water. Water levels began to recede late on the 28th into early on the 29th.
